latest.title: Transaction in Own Shares
latest.content:
9 Mar 2026 07:00
RNS Number : 7479V
McBride PLC
09 March 2026
9 March 2026
McBride plc
Transaction in Own Shares
McBride plc (the "Company") announces today it has purchased the following number of its ordinary shares of 10 pence each ("Ordinary Shares") through Peel Hunt LLP (the "Broker").
Ordinary Shares
Date of purchase
6 March 2026
Number of Ordinary Shares purchased:
65,621
Lowest price per Ordinary Share (pence):
150.00
Highest price per Ordinary Share (pence):
153.60
Volume weighted average price per day (pence):
153.17
The Company will purchase the above Ordinary Shares through the Broker on the London Stock Exchange (the "Purchases") and will subsequently cancel them.
Such Purchases form part of the Company's existing buyback programme and were effected pursuant to the non-discretionary agreement entered into with the Broker, as announced on 1st December 2025.
Following the Purchases and cancellation of these Ordinary Shares, the Company will have 176,646,736 Ordinary Shares in issue (excluding treasury shares), hold 42,041 Ordinary Shares in treasury, and have 594,610,458 non-cumulative redeemable preference shares of 0.1 pence each in the Company ("B Shares") in issue. B Shares carry no rights to attend, speak or vote at Company meetings, except on a resolution relating to the winding up of the Company.
Once the Ordinary Shares are cancelled, the total number of voting rights in the Company will be 176,646,736. This figure may be used by shareholders of the Company as the denominator for the calculations by which they can determine if they are required to notify their interest, or a change to their interest, in the Company pursuant to the Disclosure and Transparency Rules (the "DTRs").
The Company confirms that, in accordance with DTR 5.5.1R, following the Purchases the percentage of voting rights attributable to the Ordinary Shares held in treasury is 0.02 per cent.
The tables below contain detailed information about the Purchases.

company_30s.what_they_do: McBride plc makes and supplies private‑label household and professional products across Europe. Think of it as the unseen factory behind the labels on everyday items like cleaning sprays, kitchen gadgets and small appliances.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The company owns a long‑standing contract‑manufacturing network that lets retailers and brands launch new products quickly. Its scale gives it bargaining power with suppliers and a steady stream of repeat contracts, making it a reliable player in a market that values speed and cost control.
company_30s.current_state: McBride is in a growth‑phase that relies heavily on securing new contracts and expanding existing ones. The latest interim results show a rebound in operating margins after a period of cost‑inflation pressure. The stock is trading well below the price it once commanded, reflecting a market that has yet to appreciate the recent operational turnaround.

rns_narrative: The company has just released its 6‑month interim figures, which include a modest lift in revenue and a sharper improvement in gross margin.  In the same week a director change was announced, signalling a fresh strategic focus.
Two days later McBride disclosed a share buy‑back transaction, a classic sign that management believes the price is undervalued and is willing to support it.
The most recent RNS, dated 5 March, confirms that the share buy‑back was completed and that the company will continue to pay dividends once the fiscal year ends.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Quiet optimism – the community is largely silent but a few bullish posts hint at a potential rebound.
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: A handful of investors point to the buy‑back and improved margins as evidence that the company is turning around, suggesting a possible upside if the market takes notice.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Some skeptics worry that the company’s cash burn is still high and that the buy‑back could strain liquidity if sales do not accelerate.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: The chatter has moved from cautious to mildly hopeful over the last two weeks, but overall engagement remains low, keeping the noise floor very quiet.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends for McBride is flat – no recent spikes in search volume. That silence means the market is not yet primed for a surprise.

near_term_catalysts: Completion of the current share buy‑back and any subsequent tranche announcements.
A quarterly earnings release that shows continued margin improvement and a clear path to profitability.
A new contract win announced by a major retailer, which would demonstrate the company’s ability to secure high‑volume business.
A spike in trading volume that breaks the 200‑day moving average, signalling renewed investor interest.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a volume surge of at least 30% above the 20‑day average and a price break above the 52‑week low. A clear uptick in analyst coverage or a new rating upgrade would also validate the thesis.
expect_next.timing_regime: The stock is still in the early stages of a deep crash cycle. The APEX score indicates tight compression, so a breakout would likely require a catalyst that re‑establishes confidence in the business model.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: The stock is trading roughly 80% below its recent peak, giving a 2.5x upside if it recovers to 50% of that peak. Historical deep‑crash returns are over 150% on average, and the buy‑back signals that management is willing to support the price. A mean‑reversion to the 52‑week midpoint would deliver a 150% gain.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: If the company fails to secure new contracts, its cash burn could outpace revenue growth and force another buy‑back or dividend cut. A further 20% drop would wipe out most of the upside, leaving only a modest 20% profit if the price stabilises at the current level.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Potential upside: 150% (2.5x). Potential downside: 20% (0.8x). Risk‑reward ratio is roughly 3:1 – a classic asymmetry that favours a contrarian position.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter on a pullback that confirms the 200‑day moving average, allocate 10–15% of your equity per position, and set a stop 15% below the entry to limit loss to about 20% of the current price. Use a trailing stop at 10% below the peak once the rally begins.
playbook.if_watching: Hold cash until the share price breaks above the 52‑week low with at least a 30% volume spike. If the buy‑back is announced again or a new contract is signed, consider a small position to capture the early move.
playbook.if_passing: Not applicable – no imminent delisting or bankruptcy risk identified.

ai_insights.bottom_line: McBride is a deep‑crashed play with an 80% drop, a silent market that offers surprise potential, and a buy‑back catalyst that could kick off a mean‑reversion; the upside is 150% if it hits the 52‑week midpoint, while the downside is capped at 20%.
